notes: Pilot draft for language_wordplay — the most under-covered non-geography category (1 active clue at time of authoring). Clues are written only from facts in Wikipedia lead extracts stored under data/sourcing/docs/language_wordplay/. Subcategories span Definitions, Etymology, Foreign Words & Phrases, and Puns, Quotes & Idioms. Two clues draw on the same doc (umami.json, contraindication.json, taiga.json) but each cites a distinct fact from that doc.

Potential reviewer flags: (1) The 'Gary' clue relies on knowing Elbert Henry Gary's surname — verify that 'Elbert Henry' is sufficient context. (2) The 'derrick' clue does not name Thomas Derrick; reviewers may wish to decide if that is too oblique for $800. (3) 'semiquincentennial' may need aliases added if importers see scoring issues.

questions.clue: Also called mother-of-pearl, this iridescent material produced by certain molluscs lines their inner shells and forms the substance of every natural pearl.

citations.title: nacre
citations.snippet: A  shellfish  which contains  mother-of-pearl .  .mw-parser-output .defdate{font-size:smaller}  A  pearly  substance made mainly of stacked layers of  aragonite  and organic matter which lines the interior of many shells;  mother-of-pear

questions.reviewer_note: Source fact: 'Nacre, also known as mother-of-pearl, is an organic–inorganic composite material produced by some molluscs as an inner shell layer. It is also the material of which pearls are composed.' (nacre.json extract)

questions.clue: Often described as savory or brothy, this is the fifth basic taste recognized by scientists, joining sweet, sour, salty, and bitter as one of five fundamental flavors.

citations.title: umami
citations.snippet: One of the five basic  tastes , the  savory  taste of foods such as  seaweed , cured fish, aged cheeses and meats.

questions.reviewer_note: Source fact: 'Umami, or savoriness, is one of the five basic tastes.' (umami.json extract)

questions.clue: This word names the creative process of adapting factual or fictional events into a staged or filmed performance.

citations.title: dramatization
citations.snippet: The act of  dramatizing . A  version  that has been  dramatized .

questions.reviewer_note: Source fact: 'A dramatization is the creation of a dramatic performance of material depicting real or fictional events.' (a-dramatization.json extract)

questions.clue: Also called the boreal forest, this biome of conifers — pines, spruces, and larches — stretching across Siberia and Canada is the world's largest land biome.

citations.title: taiga
citations.snippet: A  subarctic   zone  of  evergreen   coniferous   forests  situated south of the  tundras  and north of the  steppes  in the  Northern Hemisphere .

questions.reviewer_note: Source fact: 'Taiga or tayga, also known as boreal forest or snow forest, is a biome characterized by coniferous forests consisting mostly of pines, spruces, and larches. The taiga, or boreal forest, is the world's largest land biome.' (taiga.json extract)

questions.clue: First called trionfi — 'triumphs' — in 15th-century Italian card games, these 78-card decks spread across Europe before French occultists in the 18th century recast them as instruments of divination.

citations.title: tarot
citations.snippet: A  card game   played  in  various   different   variations .  Any  of the  set  of 78  playing cards  ( divided  into  five   suits , including one of  permanent   trumps ),  often   used  for  mystical   divination .

questions.reviewer_note: Source fact: 'Tarot, first known as trionfi and later as tarocchi...From at least the mid-15th century, the tarot was used to play trick-taking card games...The emergence of custom decks for use in divination...began after French occultists made elaborate, but unsubstantiated, claims about their history.' (tarot-cards.json extract). Note: clue omits 'tarocchi' (stem-adjacent to 'tarot') and uses only 'trionfi' as the Italian antecedent.

questions.clue: Cognac and Armagnac from southwestern France are the most celebrated varieties of this distilled spirit, known in French as eau de vie — literally 'water of life.'

citations.title: brandy
citations.snippet: An  alcoholic   liquor   distilled  from  wine  or  fermented   fruit juice .    Any  variety  of such liquor.    A  serving  of such liquor.    To  preserve ,  flavour , or  mix  with brandy.

questions.reviewer_note: Source facts: 'Brandy is a liquor produced by distilling wine.' / 'Among the most renowned are Cognac and Armagnac from southwestern France.' / 'also called eau de vie (literally "water of life" in French).' (brandy.json extract)

questions.clue: Though this ancient Malian city actually sits just 20 kilometers north of the Niger River, its name entered English as a byword for an impossibly remote or inaccessible location.

citations.title: Timbuktu
citations.snippet: A   city   in central    Mali    .    Any proverbially distant or remote place.

questions.reviewer_note: Source fact: 'Timbuktu is an ancient city in Mali, situated 20 kilometres (12 miles) north of the Niger River.' (timbuktu.json extract). The idiomatic use of 'Timbuktu' is widely attested in English; the source grounds the city's actual location, providing the clue's contrast.

questions.clue: In clinical medicine, this term names any condition that renders a proposed treatment harmful rather than beneficial — coming in absolute and relative varieties depending on the severity of the risk.

citations.title: contraindication
citations.snippet: A  factor  or  symptom  which makes a  certain  treatment  inadvisable , generally or individually.

questions.reviewer_note: Source facts: 'a contraindication is a condition...that serves as a reason not to take a certain medical treatment due to the harm that it would cause the patient.' / 'Absolute contraindications...no reasonable circumstances for undertaking a course of action.' / 'Relative contraindications...patient is at higher risk of complications.' (contraindication.json extract)

questions.clue: Until about 1930, English speakers knew this Nordic capital by its Swedish form; today the Finnish version is standard worldwide, reflecting its status as a municipality where both languages hold official standing.

citations.title: Helsinki
citations.snippet: The  capital city  of   Finland  ; a  municipality , the  capital  of the region of   Uusimaa  ,   Finland             .    The  Finnish  government.

questions.reviewer_note: Source facts: 'Helsinki, until about 1930 known in English by its Swedish name of Helsingfors' / 'Helsinki is a bilingual municipality with Finnish and Swedish as its official languages.' (helsinki.json extract). Clue deliberately omits 'Helsingfors' to avoid the shared Helsing- root stem.

questions.clue: Borrowed from Russian into English, this loanword names the vast subarctic conifer belt encircling Earth from Siberia through Scandinavia and across the interior of Canada and Alaska.

citations.title: taiga
citations.snippet: A  subarctic   zone  of  evergreen   coniferous   forests  situated south of the  tundras  and north of the  steppes  in the  Northern Hemisphere .

questions.reviewer_note: Source fact: 'Taiga or tayga (, TY-gə; Russian: тайга́...)' — the Russian origin (тайга) is given in the article's opening, establishing this as a loanword from Russian. (taiga.json extract)

questions.clue: Founded in 1906 as a hub for U.S. Steel, this Indiana city was named for co-founder Elbert Henry — a surname that is today far more commonly encountered as an American given name.

citations.title: Gary
citations.snippet: A male  given name  from the Germanic languages, popular from the 1940s to the 1970s; variant form   Garry      .  A  surname  from Middle English [in turn originating as a patronymic]; variant form   Garry      .  Numerous places in the

questions.reviewer_note: Source fact: 'The city of Gary, Indiana, a steel town, was named for him when it was founded in 1906.' (nonconfrontational.json / Elbert Henry Gary article). Clue gives first and middle names only ('Elbert Henry') — solver must supply the surname 'Gary' which is also the city name. Reviewer should confirm this structure is acceptable as an eponym clue.

questions.clue: In 1909, Kikunae Ikeda and Saburōsuke Suzuki co-founded Ajinomoto to market the world's first commercial seasoning made from monosodium glutamate — the amino acid compound responsible for this fifth basic taste.

citations.title: umami
citations.snippet: One of the five basic  tastes , the  savory  taste of foods such as  seaweed , cured fish, aged cheeses and meats.

questions.reviewer_note: Source fact: 'in 1909, Ikeda and Saburōsuke Suzuki founded Ajinomoto Co., Inc. which introduced the world's first umami seasoning: monosodium glutamate.' (umami.json extract)

questions.clue: Also called a sestercentennial or quarter millennium, this rarely encountered word denotes a 250th anniversary — the milestone the United States will mark on July 4, 2026.

citations.title: semiquincentennial
citations.snippet: Of or  related  to a 250th  anniversary . A 250th  anniversary .

questions.reviewer_note: Source fact: 'The United States Semiquincentennial, also called the Sestercentennial (two and a half centuries), or the Quarter Millennium, will be the 250th anniversary of the United States Declaration of Independence.' (a-semiquincentennial.json extract)

questions.clue: Named for a prolific Elizabethan-era London executioner whose gallows became an unexpected eponym, this lifting frame also designates the framework supporting a drill string on an oil rig.

citations.title: derrick
citations.snippet: A device that is used for lifting and moving large objects. A  framework  that is constructed over a mine or oil well for the purpose of  boring  or lowering pipes.    An  executioner  responsible for hanging convicts; a  hangman .    To  h

questions.reviewer_note: Source fact: 'The derrick derives its name from a type of gallows named after Thomas Derrick, an Elizabethan-era English executioner.' (a-derrick.json extract). Clue avoids naming 'Thomas Derrick' directly to prevent the surname leaking the answer.

questions.clue: Children with a viral illness given aspirin exemplify the absolute form of this clinical term: the finding that aspirin could trigger Reye syndrome in such patients made it a permanent medical restriction.

citations.title: contraindication
citations.snippet: A  factor  or  symptom  which makes a  certain  treatment  inadvisable , generally or individually.

questions.reviewer_note: Source fact: 'Children and teenagers with viral infections should not be given aspirin because of the risk of Reye syndrome.' — given as an example of an absolute contraindication. (contraindication.json extract)
